evals.name: clear-multi-step-new-project
evals.prompt: Set up a project for this task: **Plan holiday to Japan** — Want to go for two weeks in October, need to sort flights, accommodation, itinerary, and travel insurance.
evals.expected_output: Should propose an outcome-oriented project name, suggest an area, propose a concrete first action, and infer active status from the October timeframe. Should not create a full project plan with subtasks.

assertions.name: proposes-outcome-name
assertions.criteria: The response proposes a project name that describes a finished state or result — NOT an activity. It should NOT start with a verb like 'Plan', 'Sort', 'Set up', 'Organise'. Good examples: 'Japan holiday booked', 'Two-week Japan trip planned and booked'. Bad examples: 'Plan holiday to Japan', 'Plan Japan trip'.

assertions.name: suggests-area
assertions.criteria: The response suggests or asks about which area (top-level parent project) the project should live under.

assertions.name: identifies-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response proposes or asks about a concrete first action — a single step the user can take to get started.

assertions.name: does-not-create-full-plan
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT create multiple subtasks, sections, or a detailed project plan. At most one first action.

evals.name: merge-with-existing-project
evals.prompt: Set up a project for this task: **Sort out home insurance renewal** — Current policy expires next month, need to compare quotes and switch provider. I already have a project called 'Home Admin' for household paperwork.
evals.expected_output: Should detect the existing 'Home Admin' project and propose merging rather than creating a new project. Should ask one question at a time — merge first, then first action later.

assertions.name: proposes-merge
assertions.criteria: The response acknowledges the existing 'Home Admin' project and suggests adding this work there rather than creating a brand new project.

assertions.name: asks-one-question
assertions.criteria: The response asks exactly one question — the merge question. It does not also ask about first action, area, or priority in the same response.

assertions.name: does-not-create-new-project
assertions.criteria: The response does not create a separate new project — it proposes using the existing one.

evals.name: someday-maybe-project
evals.prompt: Set up a project for this task: **Learn woodworking** — Always fancied making furniture. Maybe take a course or start with some YouTube tutorials. No rush.
evals.expected_output: Should recognise this as someday/maybe from the 'no rush' and 'maybe' language. Should place the project under a Someday/Maybe area.

assertions.name: identifies-someday-maybe
assertions.criteria: The response identifies this as a someday/maybe project, not an active priority. It picks up on cues like 'no rush', 'maybe', 'always fancied'.

assertions.name: places-under-someday-area
assertions.criteria: The response places or proposes placing the project under a Someday/Maybe area (or equivalent).

assertions.name: still-creates-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response still creates or proposes a first action, even for a someday/maybe project.

evals.name: outcome-needs-refining
evals.prompt: Set up a project for this task: **Sort out finances** — Things are a mess, bills everywhere, no budget, credit card debt building up.
evals.expected_output: Should ask what outcome the user is after since 'sort out finances' is too vague for a project name. Should not assume what the project is about.

assertions.name: asks-about-outcome
assertions.criteria: The response asks the user what specific outcome they're after — it doesn't just accept 'Sort out finances' as the project name.

assertions.name: asks-one-question
assertions.criteria: The response asks exactly one question, not multiple questions at once.

assertions.name: does-not-assume-scope
assertions.criteria: The response does not assume what the project should cover or create a project without clarifying the outcome first.

evals.name: urgent-active-project
evals.prompt: Set up a project for this task: **Move house** — We've exchanged contracts, completion is on April 15th. Need to sort removals, redirect mail, update address everywhere, and cancel utilities.
evals.expected_output: Should infer this is clearly active (concrete date, exchanged contracts). Should not ask about someday/maybe. Should propose outcome-oriented name and first action.

assertions.name: infers-active
assertions.criteria: The response treats this as an active project without asking about priority vs someday. The April 15th date and exchanged contracts make this obviously active.

assertions.name: proposes-outcome-name
assertions.criteria: The response proposes a project name that describes a finished state — NOT an activity. It should NOT start with a verb like 'Move', 'Sort', 'Set up'. Good examples: 'Moved into new house', 'House move completed by April 15th'. Bad examples: 'Move house', 'Complete house move'.

assertions.name: identifies-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response proposes a concrete first action.

assertions.name: does-not-create-full-plan
assertions.criteria: The response does NOT create multiple subtasks or a detailed project plan. At most one first action.

evals.name: project-with-obvious-area
evals.prompt: Set up a project for this task: **Prepare Q2 board presentation** — Need to compile financials, write the narrative, get design to make the deck, and rehearse.
evals.expected_output: Should clearly assign to a Work area without asking. Should propose outcome-oriented name and first action.

assertions.name: assigns-work-area
assertions.criteria: The response assigns or proposes the project under a Work area (or equivalent business/professional area) without needing to ask.

assertions.name: proposes-outcome-name
assertions.criteria: The response proposes a project name that describes a finished state — NOT an activity. It should NOT start with a verb like 'Prepare', 'Create', 'Make'. Good examples: 'Q2 board presentation ready', 'Q2 board deck delivered'. Bad examples: 'Prepare Q2 board presentation', 'Create board deck'.

assertions.name: identifies-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response proposes a concrete first action — a single step to get started.

assertions.name: does-not-ask-unnecessary-questions
assertions.criteria: The response does not ask questions that have obvious answers from context (e.g. doesn't ask about area when it's clearly work, doesn't ask about active vs someday for a Q2 deadline).

evals.name: outcome-not-activity-name
evals.prompt: Set up a project for this task: **Organise garage** — It's full of junk, can barely get the car in. Need to clear stuff out, put up shelving, and get some storage boxes.
evals.expected_output: Should propose an outcome-based project name describing the finished state (e.g. 'Garage cleared and organised') rather than an activity-based name (e.g. 'Organise garage', 'Clear out garage'). Should suggest area and first action.

assertions.name: name-describes-finished-state
assertions.criteria: The proposed project name describes a finished state or result — what 'done' looks like. Good examples: 'Garage cleared and organised', 'Garage usable again'. Bad examples: 'Organise garage', 'Clear out garage', 'Sort out garage'.

assertions.name: name-does-not-start-with-verb
assertions.criteria: The proposed project name does NOT start with an activity verb like 'Organise', 'Clear', 'Sort', 'Set up', 'Fix', 'Tidy', 'Clean'. Project names should not be phrased as actions.

assertions.name: suggests-area
assertions.criteria: The response suggests or asks about which area (top-level parent project) the project should live under.

assertions.name: identifies-first-action
assertions.criteria: The response proposes or asks about a concrete first action — a single step the user can take to get started.
